issues.original: The first time I ran [CODE] I expected to download an entire operating system. Instead, it finished in seconds and was only 77 MB. That seemed impossibly small for a Linux distribution. The secret is layers — and understanding how they work changes the way you think about building and shipping containers.
issues.fixed: The first time I ran [CODE], I expected to download an entire operating system. Instead, it finished in seconds and was only 77 MB. That seemed impossibly small for a Linux distribution. The secret is layers — understanding how they work changes the way you think about building and shipping containers.

issues.original: An **image** is a read-only template. It contains the filesystem, environment variables, default command, and metadata needed to create a container. Think of it as a class definition in object-oriented programming.
issues.fixed: An **image** is a read-only template containing the filesystem, environment variables, default command, and metadata needed to create a container. Think of it as a class definition in object-oriented programming.

issues.original: A **container** is a running (or stopped) instance created from an image. It has everything the image has, plus a writable layer on top and runtime state (network, process IDs, etc.). Think of it as an object instantiated from a class.
issues.fixed: A **container** is a running (or stopped) instance created from an image. It includes everything the image has, plus a writable layer and runtime state (network, process IDs, etc.). Think of it as an object instantiated from a class.

issues.original: Every Docker image is built from a stack of layers. Each layer represents a set of filesystem changes — files added, modified, or deleted. Layers are:
issues.fixed: Every Docker image is built from a stack of layers. Each layer represents filesystem changes — files added, modified, or deleted. Layers are:

issues.original: When a container starts from this image, Docker adds one more layer on top: If you modify a file from a lower layer inside the container, the union filesystem uses **copy-on-write**: it copies the file up to the writable layer, then modifies the copy. The original in the read-only layer remains unchanged.
issues.fixed: When a container starts from this image, Docker adds one more layer on top. If you modify a file from a lower layer inside the container, the union filesystem uses **copy-on-write**: it copies the file to the writable layer, then modifies the copy. The original in the read-only layer remains unchanged.

issues.original: Let's trace what happens during [CODE]: Six layers downloaded. Each [CODE] line is a separate layer. Docker downloaded them in parallel (you'd see progress bars in a real terminal). The [CODE] is the SHA256 hash of the image manifest — it uniquely identifies this exact combination of layers.
issues.fixed: Let's trace what happens during [CODE]: six layers are downloaded. Each [CODE] line is a separate layer. Docker downloads them in parallel (you'd see progress bars in a real terminal). The [CODE] is the SHA256 hash of the image manifest, which uniquely identifies this exact combination of layers.

issues.original: Now pull another image that shares the same base: Notice [CODE]. Docker recognized that it already had this layer (shared with another image, likely the Alpine base) and skipped downloading it. This is layer sharing in action — it saves both bandwidth and disk space.
issues.fixed: Now pull another image that shares the same base. Notice [CODE]. Docker recognizes it already has this layer (likely shared with the Alpine base) and skips downloading it. This is layer sharing in action, saving both bandwidth and disk space.

issues.original: For detailed metadata in JSON format: These are the content-addressable SHA256 hashes of each layer. Docker uses these to determine if a layer is already present locally.
issues.fixed: For detailed metadata in JSON format, these are the content-addressable SHA256 hashes of each layer. Docker uses them to determine if a layer is already present locally.

issues.original: Docker images follow a naming convention: Examples: | Full Name | Registry | Namespace | Repository | Tag | |-----------|----------|-----------|------------|-----| | [CODE] | docker.io (implicit) | library (implicit) | nginx | latest (implicit) | | [CODE] | docker.io | library | nginx | 1.25 | | [CODE] | docker.io | library | ubuntu | 22.04 | | [CODE] | docker.io | myuser | myapp | v2 | | [CODE] | gcr.io | project | app | prod | | [CODE] | ghcr.io | owner | repo | sha-abc123 | | [CODE] | registry.example.com:5000 | team | svc | latest |
issues.fixed: Docker images follow a naming convention. Examples: | Full Name | Registry | Namespace | Repository | Tag | |-----------|----------|-----------|------------|-----| | [CODE] | docker.io (implicit) | library (implicit) | nginx | latest (implicit) | | [CODE] | docker.io | library | nginx | 1.25 | | [CODE] | docker.io | library | ubuntu | 22.04 | | [CODE] | docker.io | myuser | myapp | v2 | | [CODE] | gcr.io | project | app | prod | | [CODE] | ghcr.io | owner | repo | sha-abc123 | | [CODE] | registry.example.com:5000 | team | svc | latest |

issues.original: Most tutorials show you a 5-line Dockerfile and move on. Then you deploy to production and discover your image is 1.2 GB, builds take 8 minutes even for a one-line code change, and your security team is flagging vulnerabilities in packages you didn't even know were installed. Writing a good Dockerfile is a skill — one that pays dividends every time your CI pipeline runs.
issues.fixed: Most tutorials show you a 5-line Dockerfile and move on. Then, when you deploy to production, you find your image is 1.2 GB, builds take 8 minutes even for a one-line code change, and your security team flags vulnerabilities in packages you didn't even know were installed. Writing a good Dockerfile is a skill that pays off every time your CI pipeline runs.

issues.fixed: If you [CODE] in one [CODE] and [CODE] in another, the package lists still exist in the first layer because layers are additive, not subtractive.

issues.fixed: Neither [CODE] nor [CODE] should contain secrets. Build arguments are stored in the image history ([CODE]). Use Docker secrets or mount secrets at build time with [CODE] instead.

issues.fixed: We'll cover why this matters for security in article 7.

issues.original: Docker marks the container as [CODE], [CODE], or [CODE] based on the healthcheck results. Orchestrators (Docker Compose, Kubernetes) use this to decide whether to route traffic to the container.
issues.fixed: Docker marks the container as [CODE], [CODE], or [CODE] based on the healthcheck results. Orchestrators like Docker Compose and Kubernetes use this to decide whether to route traffic to the container.

issues.original: The [CODE] file works like [CODE] but for the Docker build context. When you run [CODE], Docker sends the entire directory (the "build context") to the daemon. Without [CODE], this includes everything.
issues.fixed: The [CODE] file works like [CODE] but for the Docker build context. When you run [CODE], Docker sends the entire directory (the "build context") to the daemon. Without [CODE], this includes everything.

issues.original: Without a [CODE], a project with a 500 MB [CODE] directory and a 200 MB [CODE] sends 700 MB to the daemon on every build — even if none of those files are used in the image.
issues.fixed: Without a [CODE], a project with a 500 MB [CODE] directory and a 200 MB [CODE] sends 700 MB to the daemon on every build, even if none of those files are used in the image.

issues.original: This is why instruction order is critical: The general rule: order instructions from least-frequently-changing to most-frequently-changing.
issues.fixed: This is why instruction order is critical. The general rule is to order instructions from least-frequently-changing to most-frequently-changing.

issues.original: Containers are deliberately isolated. That's the point. But useful applications need to accept connections from the outside world, talk to databases, and store data that survives container restarts. Docker provides two mechanisms for this: networks (how containers communicate) and volumes (how data persists). Getting these right is the difference between a demo and a deployment.
issues.fixed: Containers are deliberately isolated. That's the point. But useful applications need to accept connections from the outside world, talk to databases, and store data that survives container restarts. Docker provides two mechanisms for this: networks (for communication) and volumes (for persistent storage). Getting these right is the difference between a demo and a deployment.

issues.original: When Docker starts, it creates a virtual network infrastructure on the host. Each container gets its own network namespace (its own IP address, routing table, and network interfaces), and Docker manages how traffic flows between containers and the outside world.
issues.fixed: When Docker starts, it creates a virtual network infrastructure on the host. Each container gets its own network namespace (with its own IP address, routing table, and network interfaces), and Docker manages the traffic flow between containers and the outside world.

issues.original: For most single-host use cases, you'll use **bridge** networks. Let's explore each relevant driver.
issues.fixed: For most single-host use cases, you'll use **bridge** networks. Let's explore the relevant drivers.

issues.original: Every container that doesn't specify a network joins the default bridge. Let's see it in action:
issues.fixed: Every container that doesn't specify a network joins the default bridge. Let's see it in action:

issues.original: This is a deliberate limitation of the default bridge. For service discovery, you need a custom bridge network.
issues.fixed: This is a deliberate limitation of the default bridge. For service discovery, you need a custom bridge network.

issues.original: Custom bridge networks provide automatic DNS resolution between containers — this is what you should use for applications:
issues.fixed: Custom bridge networks provide automatic DNS resolution between containers — this is what you should use for applications.

issues.original: The container name [CODE] resolves to its IP address. This is Docker's built-in DNS server at work. Docker runs an embedded DNS server at [CODE] inside each container on a custom network.
issues.fixed: The container name [CODE] resolves to its IP address. This is Docker's built-in DNS server at work. Docker runs an embedded DNS server at [CODE] inside each container on a custom network.

issues.original: Custom networks also provide isolation — containers on different networks can't communicate unless explicitly connected:
issues.fixed: Custom networks also provide isolation — containers on different networks can't communicate unless explicitly connected.

issues.original: To expose a container's port to the host (and thus the outside world), you map ports:
issues.fixed: To expose a container's port to the host (and thus the outside world), you map ports.

issues.original: The format is always [CODE]. Read it as "host port 8080 forwards to container port 80."
issues.fixed: The format is always [CODE]. Read it as "host port 8080 forwards to container port 80."

issues.original: The [CODE] network mode removes network isolation entirely — the container uses the host's network stack directly:
issues.fixed: The [CODE] network mode removes network isolation entirely — the container uses the host's network stack directly.

issues.original: This eliminates the overhead of port mapping and NAT, providing slightly better network performance. The tradeoff: you lose port isolation (two containers can't both listen on port 80), and the container can see all host network interfaces.
issues.fixed: This eliminates the overhead of port mapping and NAT, providing slightly better network performance. The tradeoff: you lose port isolation (two containers can't both listen on port 80), and the container can see all host network interfaces.

issues.original:
Use host networking when:
- You need maximum network performance
- Your application binds to many ports dynamically
- You're running monitoring tools that need to see host network traffic
issues.fixed:
Use host networking when:
- You need maximum network performance
- Your application binds to many ports dynamically
- You're running monitoring tools that need to see host network traffic

issues.original: By default, all data written inside a container is stored in its writable layer. When the container is deleted, that data is gone. Volumes provide persistent storage that exists independently of the container lifecycle.
issues.fixed: By default, all data written inside a container is stored in its writable layer. When the container is deleted, that data is gone. Volumes provide persistent storage that exists independently of the container lifecycle.

issues.original: Docker supports three ways to mount storage into a container:
issues.fixed: Docker supports three ways to mount storage into a container.

issues.original: Named volumes are the recommended way to persist data. Docker manages the storage location, and volumes can be shared between containers:
issues.fixed: Named volumes are the recommended way to persist data. Docker manages the storage location, and volumes can be shared between containers.

issues.original: Bind mounts map a specific host directory into the container. They're essential for development workflows:
issues.fixed: Bind mounts map a specific host directory into the container. They're essential for development workflows.

issues.original: Any changes to files on the host are immediately visible in the container (and vice versa). This enables live development without rebuilding the image.
issues.fixed: Any changes to files on the host are immediately visible in the container (and vice versa). This enables live development without rebuilding the image.

issues.original: The previous articles taught you how to run containers with [CODE], pass port mappings with [CODE], create networks with [CODE], and mount volumes with [CODE]. Now imagine doing that for a web server, an API backend, a database, a cache, and a task queue — every time you start your development environment. Docker Compose replaces those 20+ commands with a single file and a single command: [CODE].
issues.fixed: The previous articles showed you how to run containers, pass port mappings, create networks, and mount volumes. Now, imagine doing all that for a web server, an API backend, a database, a cache, and a task queue—every time you start your development environment. Docker Compose replaces those 20+ commands with a single file and a single command: [CODE].

issues.original: A typical web application has multiple services: - A frontend (React, Vue, or server-rendered HTML) - A backend API (Python, Node.js, Go) - A database (PostgreSQL, MySQL) - A cache (Redis, Memcached) - A task queue (Celery, Bull) - Maybe a reverse proxy (nginx) Each service is its own container. Without Compose, starting this stack means running [CODE], [CODE], and a separate [CODE] with a dozen flags for each service — five or more commands, easy to get wrong, impossible to version control cleanly. Compose turns this into a declarative file.
issues.fixed: A typical web application includes multiple services: - A frontend (React, Vue, or server-rendered HTML) - A backend API (Python, Node.js, Go) - A database (PostgreSQL, MySQL) - A cache (Redis, Memcached) - A task queue (Celery, Bull) - Optionally, a reverse proxy (nginx) Each service runs in its own container. Without Compose, starting this stack involves running [CODE], [CODE], and a separate [CODE] with a dozen flags for each service—five or more commands, easy to get wrong, and impossible to version control cleanly. Compose simplifies this into a declarative file.

issues.original: When you run [CODE], Compose: 1. Creates a dedicated network (named [CODE]) for all services 2. Creates named volumes declared in the [CODE] section 3. Builds images for services with [CODE] directives 4. Starts containers in dependency order 5. Attaches service names as DNS hostnames (so [CODE] can reach [CODE] by name) 6. Streams all logs to your terminal (unless [CODE] is used)
issues.fixed: When you run [CODE], Compose: 1. Creates a dedicated network (named [CODE]) for all services 2. Creates named volumes declared in the [CODE] section 3. Builds images for services with [CODE] directives 4. Starts containers in dependency order 5. Attaches service names as DNS hostnames (so [CODE] can reach [CODE] by name) 6. Streams all logs to your terminal (unless [CODE] is used)

issues.original: A container that works is invisible. A container that doesn't work is a black box. The entire point of containerization is isolation — but that same isolation makes debugging harder. You can't just [CODE] into a container or browse its filesystem from the host. Docker provides a specific set of tools for inspecting, diagnosing, and understanding what happens inside running (and crashed) containers.
issues.fixed: A working container is invisible. A non-working one is a black box. The main goal of containerization is isolation, but this isolation also makes debugging more challenging. You can't simply [CODE] into a container or browse its filesystem from the host. Docker offers a specific set of tools for inspecting, diagnosing, and understanding what happens inside running and crashed containers.

issues.original: Logs are your first line of investigation. Docker captures anything a container writes to stdout and stderr.
issues.fixed: Logs are your first line of investigation. Docker captures everything a container writes to stdout and stderr.

issues.original: This is critical — when a container crashes, it stops, but its logs are preserved until the container is removed:

issues.original: The container exited with code 1 (error). The logs reveal it couldn't connect to PostgreSQL. Maybe the database wasn't ready when the app started (a dependency ordering issue), or the hostname is wrong.
issues.fixed: The container exited with code 1 (error). The logs show it couldn't connect to PostgreSQL. This could be because the database wasn't ready when the app started (a dependency ordering issue) or the hostname was incorrect.

issues.fixed: Exit code 137 deserves special attention; it usually means the container was killed by the OOM (Out of Memory) killer for exceeding its memory limit.

issues.original: Docker's default configuration prioritizes convenience over security. Out of the box, containers run as root, have access to a broad set of Linux capabilities, and can write to their entire filesystem. This is fine for development — dangerous for production. A container escape vulnerability combined with a root-privileged container means an attacker owns the host. Let's fix that.
issues.fixed: Docker's default configuration prioritizes convenience over security. Containers run as root, have access to a broad set of Linux capabilities, and can write to their entire filesystem. This is fine for development but dangerous for production. A container escape vulnerability in a root-privileged container means an attacker can take over the host. Let's fix that.

issues.original: Before locking things down, understand what you're defending against:
issues.fixed: Before securing your setup, understand what you're defending against:

issues.original: Each hardening technique addresses one or more of these threats. The goal is defense in depth — no single measure is sufficient, but layers of hardening make exploitation significantly harder.
issues.fixed: Each hardening technique addresses one or more of these threats. The goal is defense in depth: no single measure is sufficient, but layers of hardening make exploitation much harder.

issues.original: By default, the process inside a Docker container runs as root (UID 0). This root is the same root as on the host (unless user namespaces are enabled). If an attacker escapes the container, they're root on the host.
issues.fixed: By default, the process inside a Docker container runs as root (UID 0). This root is the same as on the host (unless user namespaces are enabled). If an attacker escapes the container, they become root on the host.

issues.original: Even if the Dockerfile doesn't set a user, you can override at runtime:
issues.fixed: Even if the Dockerfile doesn't set a user, you can override it at runtime:

issues.original: Running as non-root can break things that assume root access:
issues.fixed: Running as a non-root user can break things that assume root access:

issues.original: A read-only root filesystem prevents an attacker from modifying binaries, planting malware, or changing configuration files:
issues.fixed: A read-only root filesystem prevents attackers from modifying binaries, planting malware, or changing configuration files:

issues.original: Most applications need to write to some locations (temp files, caches, pid files). Use tmpfs for writable areas:
issues.fixed: Most applications need to write to some locations (temp files, caches, pid files). Use tmpfs for these writable areas:

issues.original: Linux capabilities divide root's power into ~40 individual privileges. By default, Docker grants containers a subset of these — more than most applications need.
issues.fixed: Linux capabilities divide root's power into about 40 individual privileges. By default, Docker grants containers a subset of these, more than most applications need.

issues.original: The principle of least privilege: drop all capabilities, then add back only what your application needs.
issues.fixed: Follow the principle of least privilege: drop all capabilities and add back only what your application needs.

issues.original: Secrets (API keys, database passwords, TLS certificates) are one of the most common security failures in containerized applications.
issues.fixed: Secrets (API keys, database passwords, TLS certificates) are among the most common security failures in containerized applications.

issues.fixed: Trivy is a vulnerability scanner that checks container images against known CVE databases:

issues.original: Everything in this series so far has been single-host Docker: one machine running containers. This works well for development, small projects, and applications with modest traffic. But the moment you need your service to survive a server failure, handle traffic spikes, or deploy updates without downtime, single-host Docker shows its limits. Container orchestration solves these problems — and Kubernetes has become the de facto answer.
issues.fixed: So far, this series has focused on single-host Docker: one machine running containers. This setup works well for development, small projects, and applications with modest traffic. However, when you need your service to survive server failures, handle traffic spikes, or deploy updates without downtime, single-host Docker falls short. Container orchestration addresses these issues, and Kubernetes has become the go-to solution.

issues.original: Consider what happens when your Docker host fails:
issues.fixed: Consider what happens when your Docker host fails:

issues.original: Docker Swarm is Docker's built-in orchestration. If you know [CODE], you already know 80% of Swarm. It uses the same YAML format and similar commands.
issues.fixed: Docker Swarm is Docker's built-in orchestration. If you know [CODE], you already know 80% of Swarm. It uses the same YAML format and similar commands.

issues.original: Swarm distributed the three replicas across all three nodes. It also provides built-in load balancing — any node in the swarm can accept traffic on port 80, and Swarm routes it to a container running the service.
issues.fixed: Swarm distributes the three replicas across all three nodes. It also provides built-in load balancing: any node in the swarm can accept traffic on port 80, and Swarm routes it to a container running the service.

issues.original: Swarm updates one container at a time, waiting 10 seconds between each. If a new container fails its healthcheck, Swarm rolls back automatically.
issues.fixed: Swarm updates one container at a time, waiting 10 seconds between each. If a new container fails its health check, Swarm rolls back automatically.

issues.original: Kubernetes (K8s) is the dominant container orchestration platform. It's more complex than Swarm but dramatically more capable. Most cloud providers offer managed Kubernetes services (EKS, GKE, AKS, ACK), eliminating the operational burden of managing the control plane.
issues.fixed: Kubernetes (K8s) is the dominant container orchestration platform. It's more complex than Swarm but much more capable. Most cloud providers offer managed Kubernetes services (EKS, GKE, AKS, ACK), eliminating the operational burden of managing the control plane.

issues.original: The architecture looks like this (described, not drawn):
issues.fixed: The architecture looks like this (described, not drawn):

issues.original: A Pod contains one or more containers that share network and storage. You rarely create pods directly. Instead, you use higher-level objects.
issues.fixed: A Pod contains one or more containers that share network and storage. You rarely create pods directly; instead, you use higher-level objects.
